Details: Both stored procedures implement data quality validation for MEMBERSDATA table with similar rule sets. Agent_1 uses external Snowflake connection approach while Agent_2 uses internal Snowflake JavaScript API with better modularity and error handling. Key differences include connection methodology (external vs internal), error handling sophistication, and code organization (monolithic vs modular functions).

Both procedures address the same core objective of running DQ validation checks on MEMBERSDATA table and logging violations. They implement identical rule sets covering null checks, format validation, uniqueness, and range validation. Primary semantic difference is in execution approach - Agent_1 uses external connection while Agent_2 uses internal Snowflake context. Both generate batch IDs and log violations to DQ_ISSUE_LOG table.

Significant structural differences exist. Agent_1 follows monolithic approach with single main execution block (lines 20-65), while Agent_2 uses modular design with separate functions: ensure_batch_id_sequence(), get_new_batch_id(), dq_insert(), dq_bulk_insert(), escape_sql_literal(), and run_checks() (lines 15-180). Both use similar rule iteration patterns but Agent_2 provides better separation of concerns and reusability.

Syntax issues include: external Snowflake SDK usage not supported in Snowflake stored procedures (lines 20-25), incorrect result handling for connection.execute() method (line 45), and missing proper error handling for connection failures. REGEXP_MATCHES syntax may not be valid in all Snowflake versions (lines 55-58).

Minor syntax issue with RLIKE usage which should be REGEXP for Snowflake compatibility (line 140, 144, 148). Otherwise, code follows proper Snowflake JavaScript stored procedure patterns with correct API usage, proper error handling, and valid SQL constructs.
